James IV put the enterprise on a new footing, founding a harbour at [Newhaven](/wiki/Newhaven,_Edinburgh "Newhaven, Edinburgh") and a dockyard at the Pools of [Airth](/wiki/Airth "Airth").[[144]](#cite_note-151) He acquired a total of 38 ships including the *[Great Michael](/wiki/Great_Michael "Great Michael")*,[[145]](#cite_note-Smout-152) at that time, the largest ship in Europe.[[145]](#cite_note-Smout-152)[[146]](#cite_note-Murdoch-p33-34-153) Scottish ships had some success against privateers, accompanied the king on his expeditions in the islands and intervened in conflicts in Scandinavia and the Baltic,[[143]](#cite_note-Grant-150) but were sold after the [Flodden campaign](/wiki/Battle_of_Flodden "Battle of Flodden") and after 1516 Scottish naval efforts would rely on privateering captains and hired merchantmen.[[143]](#cite_note-Grant-150) James V did not share his father's interest in developing a navy and shipbuilding fell behind that of the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries").[[147]](#cite_note-154) Despite truces between England and Scotland there were periodic outbreaks of a *[guerre de course](/wiki/Guerre_de_course "Guerre de course")*.[[148]](#cite_note-155) James V built a new harbour at [Burntisland](/wiki/Burntisland "Burntisland") in 1542.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas2005164-156) The chief use of naval power in his reign was a series of expeditions to the Isles and France.[[150]](#cite_note-Dawson-p76-157) After the [Union of Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_Crowns "Union of Crowns") in 1603 conflict between Scotland and England ended, but Scotland found itself involved in England's foreign policy, opening up Scottish shipping to attack. In 1626, a squadron of three ships was bought and equipped.[[146]](#cite_note-Murdoch-p33-34-153) There were also several [marque fleets](/wiki/Letter_of_marque "Letter of marque") of privateers.[[151]](#cite_note-158) In 1627, the [Royal Scots Navy](/wiki/Royal_Scots_Navy "Royal Scots Navy") and accompanying contingents of burgh privateers participated in the [major expedition to Biscay](/wiki/Siege_of_Saint-Martin-de-R%C3%A9_(1627) "Siege of Saint-Martin-de-Ré (1627)").[[152]](#cite_note-159) The Scots also returned to the West Indies[[153]](#cite_note-160) and in 1629 took part in the capture of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec").[[154]](#cite_note-161)

During the Bishop's Wars the king attempted to blockade Scotland and planned amphibious assaults from England on the East coast and from Ireland to the West.[[155]](#cite_note-Wheeler2002pp19-21-162) Scottish privateers took a number of English prizes.[[156]](#cite_note-163) After the Covenanters allied with the English Parliament they established two patrol squadrons for the Atlantic and North Sea coasts, known collectively as the "Scotch Guard".[[157]](#cite_note-164) The Scottish navy was unable to withstand the English fleet that accompanied the army led by Cromwell that conquered Scotland in 1649–1651 and the Scottish ships and crews were split up among the Commonwealth fleet.[[158]](#cite_note-165) Scottish seamen received protection against arbitrary impressment by English men of war, but a fixed quota of conscripts for the Royal Navy was levied from the sea-coast [burghs](/wiki/Burgh "Burgh") during the second half of the 17th century.[[159]](#cite_note-166) Royal Navy patrols were now found in Scottish waters even in peacetime.[[160]](#cite_note-167) In the [Second](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Dutch_War "Second Anglo-Dutch War") (1665–1667) and [Third Anglo-Dutch Wars](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Dutch_War "Third Anglo-Dutch War") (1672–1674) between 80 and 120 captains, took Scottish letters of marque and privateers played a major part in the naval conflict.[[161]](#cite_note-168) In the 1690s, a small fleet of five ships was established by merchants for the [Darien Scheme](/wiki/Darien_Scheme "Darien Scheme"),[[162]](#cite_note-169) and a professional navy was established for the protection of commerce in home waters during the Nine Years' War, with three purpose-built warships bought from English shipbuilders in 1696. After the [Act of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") in 1707, these vessels were transferred to the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy").[[163]](#cite_note-170)

### Army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Scotland&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Army")]

Main articles: [Scots Army](/wiki/Scots_Army "Scots Army"), [Warfare in Medieval Scotland](/wiki/Warfare_in_Medieval_Scotland "Warfare in Medieval Scotland"), and [Warfare in early modern Scotland](/wiki/Warfare_in_early_modern_Scotland "Warfare in early modern Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Scottish_soldiers_in_the_14thC.jpg/250px-Scottish_soldiers_in_the_14thC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scottish_soldiers_in_the_14thC.jpg)

Scottish soldiers in the period of the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"), detail from an edition of Froissart's Chronicles

Before the [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Scotland in the Wars of the Three Kingdoms") in the mid-17th century, there was no [standing army](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army") in the Kingdom of Scotland. In the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Early_Middle_Ages "Scotland in the Early Middle Ages"), war in Scotland was characterised by the use of small war-bands of household troops often engaging in raids and low level warfare.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlcock200356-171) By the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_High_Middle_Ages "Scotland in the High Middle Ages"), the [kings of Scotland](/wiki/Kings_of_Scotland "Kings of Scotland") could command forces of tens of thousands of men for short periods as part of the "common army", mainly of poorly armoured spear and bowmen. After the "[Davidian Revolution](/wiki/Davidian_Revolution "Davidian Revolution")" of the 12th century, which introduced elements of feudalism to Scotland, these forces were augmented by small numbers of mounted and heavily armoured knights. These armies rarely managed to stand up to the usually larger and more professional armies produced by England, but they were used to good effect by Robert I at the Battle of Bannockburn in 1314 to secure Scottish independence.[[165]](#cite_note-172) After the [Wars of Scottish Independence](/wiki/Wars_of_Scottish_Independence "Wars of Scottish Independence"), the [Auld Alliance](/wiki/Auld_Alliance "Auld Alliance") between Scotland and France played a large part in the country's military activities, especially during the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"). In the [Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Late_Middle_Ages "Scotland in the Late Middle Ages"), under the [Stewart kings](/wiki/Royal_House_of_Stewart "Royal House of Stewart") forces were further augmented by specialist troops, particularly [men-at-arms](/wiki/Men-at-arms "Men-at-arms") and [archers](/wiki/Archery "Archery"), hired by bonds of *[manrent](/wiki/Manrent "Manrent")*, similar to English [indentures](/wiki/Indentures "Indentures") of the same period.[[166]](#cite_note-173) Archers became much sought after as mercenaries in French armies of the 15th century in order to help counter the English superiority in this arm, becoming a major element of the French royal guards as the [Garde Écossaise](/wiki/Garde_%C3%89cossaise "Garde Écossaise").[[167]](#cite_note-174) The Stewarts also adopted major innovations in continental warfare, such as longer pikes and the extensive use of artillery. However, in the early 16th century one of the best armed and largest Scottish armies ever assembled still met with defeat at the hands of an English army at [the Battle of Flodden Field](/wiki/The_Battle_of_Flodden_Field "The Battle of Flodden Field") in 1513, which saw the destruction of a large number of ordinary troops, a large section of the nobility and the king, [James IV](/wiki/James_IV_of_Scotland "James IV of Scotland").[[168]](#cite_note-175) In the 16th century, the crown took an increasing role in the supply of military equipment.[[169]](#cite_note-176) The pike began to replace the spear and the Scots began to convert from the bow to gunpowder firearms.[[170]](#cite_note-177) The feudal heavy cavalry had begun to disappear from Scottish armies and the Scots fielded relatively large numbers of light horse, often drawn from the [borders](/wiki/Scottish_Borders "Scottish Borders").[[171]](#cite_note-178) James IV brought in experts from France, Germany and the Netherlands and established a gun foundry in 1511.[[150]](#cite_note-Dawson-p76-157) Gunpowder weaponry fundamentally altered the nature of castle architecture from the mid-15th century.[[172]](#cite_note-179)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Scottish_mercenaries_in_the_Thirty_Years_War.jpg/250px-Scottish_mercenaries_in_the_Thirty_Years_War.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scottish_mercenaries_in_the_Thirty_Years_War.jpg)

The earliest image of Scottish soldiers wearing [tartan](/wiki/Tartan "Tartan"); 1631 German engraving.

In the early 17th century, relatively large numbers of Scots took service in foreign armies involved in the [Thirty Years War](/wiki/Thirty_Years_War "Thirty Years War").[[173]](#cite_note-180) As armed conflict with Charles I in the Bishop's Wars became likely, hundreds of Scots mercenaries returned home from foreign service, including experienced leaders like [Alexander](/wiki/Alexander_Leslie,_1st_Earl_of_Leven "Alexander Leslie, 1st Earl of Leven") and [David Leslie](/wiki/David_Leslie,_Lord_Newark "David Leslie, Lord Newark") and these veterans played an important role in training recruits.[[155]](#cite_note-Wheeler2002pp19-21-162) These systems would form the basis of the Covenanter armies that intervened in the Civil Wars in England and Ireland.[[174]](#cite_note-181) Scottish infantry were generally armed, as was almost universal in Western Europe, with a combination of pike and shot. Scottish armies may also have had individuals with a variety of weapons including bows, [Lochaber axes](/wiki/Lochaber_axe "Lochaber axe"), and [halberds](/wiki/Halberd "Halberd").[[175]](#cite_note-182) Most cavalry were probably equipped with pistols and swords, although there is some evidence that they included lancers.[[176]](#cite_note-183) Royalist armies, like those led by [James Graham, Marquis of Montrose](/wiki/James_Graham,_1st_Marquis_of_Montrose "James Graham, 1st Marquis of Montrose") (1643–1644) and in [Glencairn's rising](/wiki/Glencairn%27s_rising "Glencairn's rising") (1653–1654) were mainly composed of conventionally armed infantry with pike and shot.[[177]](#cite_note-184) Montrose's forces were short of heavy artillery suitable for siege warfare and had only a small force of cavalry.[[178]](#cite_note-185)

At the Restoration the Privy Council established a force of several infantry regiments and a few troops of horse and there were attempts to found a national militia on the English model. The standing army was mainly employed in the suppression of Covenanter rebellions and the guerilla war undertaken by the [Cameronians](/wiki/Cameronian "Cameronian") in the East.[[179]](#cite_note-Furgol2001pp637-82-186) Pikemen became less important in the late 17th century and after the introduction of the [socket bayonet](/wiki/Socket_bayonet "Socket bayonet") disappeared altogether, while matchlock muskets were replaced by the more reliable [flintlock](/wiki/Flintlock "Flintlock").[[179]](#cite_note-Furgol2001pp637-82-186) On the eve of the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution"), the standing army in Scotland was about 3,000 men in various regiments and another 268 veterans in the major garrison towns.[[180]](#cite_note-187) After the Glorious Revolution the Scots were drawn into [King William II](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England")'s continental wars, beginning with the [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War") in Flanders (1689–1697).[[181]](#cite_note-188) By the time of the [Act of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"), the Kingdom of Scotland had a [standing army](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army") of seven units of infantry, two of horse and one troop of [Horse Guards](/wiki/Household_Cavalry "Household Cavalry"), besides varying levels of fortress artillery in the garrison castles of Edinburgh, [Dumbarton](/wiki/Dumbarton_Castle "Dumbarton Castle"), and Stirling, which would be incorporated into the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army").[[182]](#cite_note-189)

The earliest recorded use of the [Lion Rampant](/wiki/Royal_Banner_of_Scotland "Royal Banner of Scotland") as a royal emblem in Scotland was by [Alexander II](/wiki/Alexander_II_of_Scotland "Alexander II of Scotland") in 1222.[[183]](#cite_note-mcandrew-190) It is recorded with the additional [embellishment](/wiki/Embellishment "Embellishment") of a [double border](/wiki/Ordinary_(heraldry)#Subordinaries "Ordinary (heraldry)") set with [lilies](/wiki/Fleur-de-lis "Fleur-de-lis") during the reign of [Alexander III](/wiki/Alexander_III_of_Scotland "Alexander III of Scotland") (1249–1286).[[183]](#cite_note-mcandrew-190) This [emblem](/wiki/Charge_(heraldry) "Charge (heraldry)") occupied the [shield](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)") of the [royal coat of arms](/wiki/Royal_coat_of_arms_of_Scotland "Royal coat of arms of Scotland") which, together with a royal [banner](/wiki/Banner "Banner") displaying the same, was used by the King of Scots until the Union of the Crowns in 1603.[[184]](#cite_note-191) Then it was incorporated into both the royal [arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") and royal banners of successive [Scottish](/wiki/List_of_Scottish_monarchs "List of Scottish monarchs") then [British](/wiki/List_of_British_monarchs "List of British monarchs") monarchs in order to symbolise Scotland; as can be seen today in the [Royal Standard of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Royal_Standard_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal Standard of the United Kingdom").[[185]](#cite_note-192) Although now officially restricted to use by representatives of the Sovereign and at royal residences, the Royal Standard of Scotland continues to be one of Scotland's most recognisable symbols.[[186]](#cite_note-193)

According to legend, the [apostle](/wiki/Apostles_in_the_New_Testament "Apostles in the New Testament") and [martyr](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr") [Saint Andrew](/wiki/Andrew_the_Apostle "Andrew the Apostle"), the [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of Scotland, was [crucified](/wiki/Crucifixion "Crucifixion") on an X-shaped [cross](/wiki/Cross "Cross") at [Patras](/wiki/Patras "Patras") (Patrae) in [Achaea](/wiki/Achaea "Achaea").[[187]](#cite_note-NAS-194) Use of the familiar iconography of his martyrdom, showing the apostle bound to an X-shaped cross, first appears in the Kingdom of Scotland in 1180 during the reign of [William I](/wiki/William_I_of_Scotland "William I of Scotland"). This image was again depicted on [seals](/wiki/Seal_(emblem) "Seal (emblem)") used during the late 13th century; including on one particular example used by the [Guardians of Scotland](/wiki/Guardians_of_Scotland "Guardians of Scotland"), dated 1286.[[187]](#cite_note-NAS-194) Use of a simplified symbol associated with Saint Andrew which does not depict his image, namely the [saltire](/wiki/Saltire "Saltire"), or crux decussata (from the Latin crux, 'cross', and decussis, 'having the shape of the Roman numeral X'), has its origins in the late 14th century; the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") decreed in 1385 that Scottish soldiers wear a white Saint Andrew's Cross on their person, both in front and behind, for the purpose of identification.[[188]](#cite_note-Bartram-195) The earliest reference to the Saint Andrew's Cross as a flag is to be found in the *Vienna Book of Hours*, c. 1503, where a white saltire is depicted with a red background.[[188]](#cite_note-Bartram-195) In the case of Scotland, use of a blue background for the Saint Andrew's Cross is said to date from at least the 15th century,[[189]](#cite_note-196) with the first certain illustration of a flag depicting such appearing in [Sir David Lyndsay of the Mount](/wiki/David_Lyndsay "David Lyndsay")'s *Register of Scottish Arms,* c. 1542.[[190]](#cite_note-SCRAN-197)

Following the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns") in 1603, [James VI, King of Scots](/wiki/James_VI_of_Scotland "James VI of Scotland"), commissioned new designs for a banner incorporating the flags of the Kingdom of Scotland and [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"). In 1606, a [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag") was commissioned, combining the crosses of [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George") (the [Flag of England](/wiki/Flag_of_England "Flag of England")), with that of Saint Andrew.[[191]](#cite_note-198) There was also a [*Scottish* version](/wiki/Union_Flag#Scottish_Union_Flag "Union Flag") of this flag, in which the cross of Saint Andrew overlaid the cross of St George. This design may have seen limited, unofficial use in Scotland until 1707, when the *English* variant of the same, whereby the cross of St George overlaid that of St Andrew, was adopted as the flag of the unified [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain").[[192]](#cite_note-199)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Scotland&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** 'In Defens' is written, which is an abbreviation of 'In my defens God me defend' (When God protects me, God protects me), the motto of the King of Scotland. A thistle, Scotland's national flower, is planted, and below it is written in Latin, [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *'Nemo me impune lacessit'* (There is no one who harms me and goes unpunished), the motto of the King of Scotland and the motto of the Order of the Thistle.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** The [Pictish](/wiki/Pictish "Pictish") and [Cumbric languages](/wiki/Cumbric_language "Cumbric language") became extinct during the 10th and 11th centuries.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") (950–1066), [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") (1066–1550), [Modern English](/wiki/Modern_English "Modern English") (1550–1707). Overall, [English](/wiki/Scottish_English "Scottish English") began to have increased influence in Scotland from the mid-16th century.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") (until 1066), [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") (1066–13th century), [Early Scots](/wiki/Early_Scots "Early Scots") (13th century–1450), [Middle Scots](/wiki/Middle_Scots "Middle Scots") (from 1450)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Became the chief language of governance in the eleventh and twelfth centuries and was widely spoken in Scotland at the height of the [Auld Alliance](/wiki/Auld_Alliance "Auld Alliance").
6. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** Widely used for administrative and liturgical purposes.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-11)** [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic_language "Scottish Gaelic language"): *Rìoghachd na h-Alba*; [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language"): *Kinrick o Scotland*; [Norn](/wiki/Norn_language "Norn language"): *Kongungdum Skotland*
